Even US 10Y bond yields (US10Y), sometimes regarded as an excellent, real-time market indicator of long-term inflation expectations, have given up ground since March 27th, after the deadlines were announced. 10Y bond yields had begun rising in the early days of the war as broader markets began to price in the risks of elevated post-war inflation.

To me, such price action potentially signals that markets have either already bottomed or are in the process of bottoming soon.

This kind of price action is very similar to what we saw in 2025. Markets were incredibly volatile in February and March last year, unable to navigate through the tariff tantrums as the landscape for geopolitical trade kept swinging from positive to negative and vice versa. However, in April last year, the markets sensed initial signs of stability in the U.S. administration’s tariff policy after a 90-day pause in the reciprocal tariffs was announced and eventually bottomed out on April 4th, 2025. Then, throughout April last year, markets proceeded to tune out the noise, heading out of correction territory later in the same month, as illustrated in Exhibit A, despite the US and China not agreeing in principle on resolving their trade disputes at the time. Despite the US/China trade differences, the markets kept moving higher and had begun to price in the risks of the US/China trade dispute in April last year and were expecting these disputes to resolve over those 90 days.

At the moment, I suspect, markets already have or are looking ahead to a global scenario where it's back to business as usual and all parties to this war are able to find consensus on resolving their respective geopolitical disputes, at least for now. I suspect markets are using the 2-3 week deadline that President Trump announced a few days ago to anchor their expectations of thawing geopolitical tensions. Iran itself has begun to strike a more conciliatory tone, coinciding with the U.S. administration’s intention to resolve this dispute within the next 2-3 weeks.

Additionally, markets might also be signaling their intentions to look ahead to a strong roster of exciting IPOs on the horizon. With SpaceX’s $1.75 trillion IPO set for June, and IPOs of OpenAI and Anthropic expected to follow, markets are saying they want the focus to be on the business of capital flow and not on geopolitical instability.

Remember, the S&P 500 is trading at extremely reasonable valuations of just ~20x this year’s projected earnings of $320 per share. That implies projected earnings growth of a massive 18% this year. This kind of high-teens earnings growth has never been seen in the last 5 years, yet markets are trading at a forward valuation of 20x, which is equal to the 5-year PE average of 19.9x and 5% ahead of the 10-year average of 18.9x.

To me, the price action seen in the S&P 500 resembles the setup seen in 2025, and I believe markets have found a way out of the rapidly evolving geopolitical tensions.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

To quote Mark Twain, "History doesn't repeat itself, but it often rhymes.” While my analysis appears to show market action seen so far in CY26 resembling that in CY25, there could be events that could break the pattern.

The elevated risks of geopolitical tensions and conflicts could escalate even further if consensus between parties is not reached. Reports about US fighter jet/jets being shot down and a ceasefire deal falling apart raise the stakes and bring in volatility, leading to more possible disruptions in markets. I will continue to deploy my portfolio capital towards stocks on my watchlist if markets fall further since I continue to expect talks for a ceasefire to eventually land on some form of agreement to end the war. I am still looking at these escalations to end in the next 2-3 weeks, latest by the end of next month.

Additionally, the upcoming midterm elections in the US will likely affect the trajectory of the markets.

Takeaway

Despite the volatility seen so far, I believe markets are finding a way to see through the rapidly changing geopolitical conflict-driven narrative, expecting the tensions to resolve soon. This is similar to the economic trade disputes that arose out of last year’s tariff tantrums, which pressured markets initially but eventually led to markets leading up through the year.

I believe the S&P 500 still looks very appealing given its history and increased projections of earnings. I continue to be bullish on US stocks and am deploying my cash accordingly.

data.title: Starwood Property Trust: The Market Is Handing You An 11% Yield At A Deep Discount
data.description:
I’ve been watching Starwood Property Trust (STWD) trade lower for the better part of a year, and at some point a decision has to be made. I believe that the market has missed the investment narrative on STWD, as it's now sitting around $17.20 per share, which is over a 10% discount to its undepreciated book value of $19.25. It’s below what new investors paid just seven months ago when the company raised equity at $19.70 per share, as the share price has fallen to the point where the dividend yield is exceeding 11%. I think this is an interesting opportunity for long-term investors, as they can allocate capital towards one of the largest commercial mortgage REITs while everyone else is figuring out if the business case is still viable. STWD just reported its Q4 and 2025 year end numbers, which I felt did a great job of setting the stage for the future. STWD deployed $12.7 billion across their platform, which was the second largest investment year in company history, while executing $4.4 billion in capital markets transactions. STWD brought their leverage down while the board authorized a $400 million share buyback, which projects confidence. I think too many people are getting scared by the short-term geopolitical and macroeconomic implications rather than looking at what is likely to happen when the fog of war evaporates. I am adding to my position, and I will gladly continue to lock in a dividend yield that exceeds 11% from STWD when everyone else is running for the hills.

Following up on my previous article about STWD

Back in the middle of December, I had written an article on STWD (can be read here) where I discussed how I continued to buy shares whenever STWD’s dividend yield exceeded 10%. I felt that STWD’s diversified capital deployment, which included $10.2B invested YTD and a major net lease acquisition, positioned them for earnings growth as rates declined. Management's anticipation of a lower rate environment was certainly bullish, but now things have changed. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am still bullish on STWD despite a macroeconomic landscape that has changed in a way that isn’t as bullish for REITs. The chances of a rate cut this year have almost disappeared, and the spike in oil is causing input prices to increase. Despite these headwinds, I am still bullish and buying, as I believe this is a long-term opportunity to lock in a tremendous amount of yield.

I believe the macroeconomic environment is creating a great setup for STWD

The pullback in STWD has almost nothing to do with the company itself. It’s a macro story revolving around interest rates and oil. Since Liberation Day in April 2025 the market has gone through wave after wave of tariff-related anxiety. Trade policy uncertainty has become the default excuse for selling anything with yield. REITs across the board got hit, and even agency mREITs with zero credit risk sold off. Here is the big thing that many investors and the market are missing. STWD is a lender, and its borrowers are multifamily landlords collecting rent in Dallas and London, industrial operators running warehouses in New York and Dublin, and power plants generating electricity in the Northeast. These are not businesses that get disrupted because the U.S. slaps a 20% duty on European steel. The connection between trade policy and STWD’s cash flow is minimal, yet the stock has traded as though there’s a direct hit to earnings. What tariffs could do is slow economic growth more broadly, and while that’s certainly a concern, STWD’s portfolio is positioned defensively. STWD’s multifamily is now 39% of commercial loan collateral, which is bullish because people still need apartments in a recession.

What drives me crazy about how the market prices mortgage REITs is that investors hear that rates are staying higher for longer and immediately sell anything that looks like it has interest rate exposure. That’s backwards thinking for STWD as 99% of STWD’s $16.6 billion commercial lending portfolio is floating rate. When SOFR stays elevated, STWD collects more spread income. The company earns more, not less, in a higher-rate environment. STWD’s $2.3 billion residential lending portfolio is the rate-sensitivity hedge because when rates do eventually come down, those fixed-rate mortgage assets appreciate in value. In 2025 the residential book saw $122 million in positive fair value adjustments as rates declined. This indicates that STWD is structured to benefit whether rates go up or down, and the market just hasn’t bothered to look that deep into them.

I believe that 2025 was a transition year for STWD and 2026 will be the inflection year

Barry Sternlicht was upfront on the earnings call that 2025 was a transition year for STWD. The Fundamental net lease acquisition in July created temporary dilution, while the fundamental net lease acquisition in July created temporary dilution. STWD’s securitization activity and takeout refinancing led to higher-than-normal cash balances sitting on the sideline. STWD’s full year distributable earnings came in at $1.69 per share compared to the $1.92 that gets paid out through the dividend, which is tight. This included roughly $0.03 per share of temporary dilution from the FIP net lease acquisition, and there were several embedded catalysts that should drive DE higher in 2026. What really stands out is the scale of activity: STWD deployed $12.7 billion in a single year while simultaneously executing $4.4 billion in capital markets transactions. STWD isn’t treading water and trying to stay afloat as they're building earning power for the next several years. The commercial lending book grew to $16.6 billion, and the infrastructure lending book sits at $2.9 billion with an 8.1% unlevered yield. Every dollar STWD’s deploying now is a dollar earning spread income going forward.

STWD held elevated levels of cash through 2025 because of the pace of securitizations and refinancings. That’s capital that was sitting in the bank earning interest from money markets rather than being deployed into 7-8% yielding loans. As management redeploys this capital, their earnings will increase. STWD’s cash drag is temporary, and the deployment pipeline is active. When STWD originates a transitional loan, it doesn’t fund the entire amount on day one, as there are committed but unfunded amounts tied to borrower business plans. This can include renovation milestones, and lease-up thresholds. As these fundings occur, they convert idle capacity into interest-earning assets at existing spread levels, which becomes an organic growth engine that requires no new originations.

The Fundamental net lease acquisition closed in July 2025 and the platform now has 492 properties across 44 states, which are 100% occupied with a 17.3-year weighted average lease term and 2.27% annual rent escalators. In Q4 STWD acquired 16 additional properties at a 7.44% cap rate. After Q4 ended, STWD completed their second ABS transaction at a record 5.06% weighted average fixed rate. This platform is being financed at increasingly attractive levels, and the spread between acquisition cap rates and financing costs is widening. The net lease business was dilutive in the first two quarters post-acquisition, but it’s designed to be a long-duration earnings compounder. I believe that in the next several quarters it will become earnings accretive in a meaningful way.

STWD has $1.04 billion in nonaccrual assets and $624 million in foreclosed properties. These are real estate assets that currently contribute nothing to distributable earnings. STWD has been working out distressed real estate for over 30 years, and once they take control of an asset, they have a roadmap for stabilizing and monetizing it. In Q4 they sold a Woodstar Fund property at their GAAP fair value basis of $56 million for a $24 million distributable earnings gain. Two additional properties were foreclosed before the year ended, and each time that STWD takes an asset from nonaccrual to productive use is an incremental earnings event. STWD is also trading below book value, and there is a $400 million buyback authorization. At the current prices, each share repurchased is immediately accretive to book value per share and distributable earnings per share. The current authorization represents roughly 6% of STWD’s market cap, and it sends the message that management believes STWD is cheap.

STWD’s dividend exceeds 11%, and I have been compounding it into a larger position

Some investors may see STWD’s yield exceeding 11% and run because they have been programmed to think something is broken or the dividend is unsustainable at these levels. STWD has never decreased the dividend and has paid $0.48 per quarter since Q1 2014. Nothing is broken, and STWD has been able to maintain this dividend for more than a decade throughout many difficult environments, including the pandemic and rising interest rates. The yield is elevated not because the dividend is at risk but because the stock price has been compressed by sector-wide fear. When you look at the 10-year average yield of roughly 9.6%, the current 11% tells me that the market is pricing in more risk than normal. I think that risk premium is overdone, and I have been adding to my position to lock in this yield.

I started my position in STWD at the end of 2016 and since then I have received 36 consecutive dividends that have been reinvested. I have been adding to STWD but in other accounts, I have been able to audit my first block of shares to see what the actual metrics are. I started with a purchase of 135 shares, and now I have 311.50 in this account as my share count has grown by 130.74%, or 176.50 shares. My quarterly dividend payments have grown by $80.90 (124.85%) as they have increased from $64.80 to $145.70. I have generated $3,576.18 in dividend income, which is 116.90% of my original investment. My average annualized income was $259.20 when I started this investment in this account. It’s now $598.08 and I am projecting that by the end of 2035 it will be $1,470.06. The dividend continues to grow each quarter by roughly 2.34%, even though STWD isn’t increasing the dividend. I am very bullish on STWD as an income generator, and that’s why I continue to add to my position in other accounts.

The risks of investing in STWD

Just because I am bullish on STWD doesn’t mean that there aren’t risk factors to consider. Within their portfolio, office exposure remains the single biggest risk even though STWD has reduced their commercial book. If the office market deteriorates further, STWD could face additional credit provisions or property impairments despite deleveraging from this space. STWD’s dividend coverage is also in question considering that the annualized dividend of $1.92 wasn’t fully covered by their distributable earnings in 2025. If STWD’s cash deployment slows down and their nonaccrual resolutions take longer than expected, the market may continue to punish shares going forward. Also, if oil stays higher for longer and the Fed increases the chances for a rate hike, I would expect the narrative of a recession to be talked about and the market to sell STWD off more. While there are significant risks, STWD has been here before, and I am excited to add to my position as STWD is now generating a dividend yield that exceeds 11%.

Conclusion

I have been invested in STWD since the end of 2016 and have been covering it for quite some time. I can’t remember a time when the gap between the stock price and the underlying business quality was this wide. The macroeconomic environment is creating a classic dislocation between the stock price and its fundamentals. I don’t believe that STWD is a speculative bet on commercial real estate recovery, as I feel the way to look at STWD is as a diversified, multi-cylinder finance company run by a management team that’s operated through every real estate cycle since 1991. At these levels, investors are getting a dividend yield that exceeds 11%, more than a 10% discount on undepreciated book value, with an authorized buyback of $400 million. This is the kind of entry point that I like, and other income investors may look back on and wish they’d taken advantage of the opportunity.
